checks.finding: requirements.md REQ-002's 'Effort-resolution priority' remedy (added for round-1 Major-1) fully disambiguates `--role` vs `--effort-policy` but leaves a parallel, untouched ambiguity: `--requested-effort` combined with `--effort-policy welded`. The priority-order clarification states 'under `--effort-policy welded`, effort is ALWAYS the welded/v1-equivalent value' and explicitly calls out only `role_defaults`' effort component as inert in that mode -- it never mentions `--requested-effort`. Yet the same REQ-002 flag description and AC-010 describe `--requested-effort` as an unconditional 'explicit override' with no policy-scoping, and the 4-step priority ladder in Field Definitions is explicitly scoped to 'under `matrix` policy' only, leaving welded+`--requested-effort` undefined. Two implementers would reasonably diverge: one treats `--requested-effort` as silently inert under welded (matching the unqualified 'ALWAYS'), the other treats it as an unconditional override (matching AC-010's plain text and the golden-baseline scope of AC-007, which is only tested with no `--requested-effort` present). This belongs to spec review because it is the same class of flag-interaction gap round 1 already required fixing for `--role`, just left open for a sibling flag; the byte-identical golden guarantee (AC-007) and the override guarantee (AC-010) cannot both be verified as stated until this combination is resolved. Major, not Critical: it affects a secondary flag-combination, not the primary invocation path, and both candidate readings are internally coherent -- just undisambiguated.

requirements.md's Roles and Permissions section states the Agent directly edits `.github/workflows/test.yml` ('...plus edits to tests/run-all.sh/.ps1, .github/workflows/test.yml, tests/validate-repository.ps1, ... none of which are protected-gate files'); Main Workflows T-001 instructs 'wire into run-all/test.yml'; AC-027/TEST-027 require direct registration edits to `.github/workflows/test.yml`; and the Assumptions section asserts this file 'remain[s] outside the protected-gate table for the duration of this feature' with no contingency clause (unlike the adjacent A3 assumption, which explicitly provides for re-verification against drift). Per context supplied for this round, a concurrent merge landed between round 1 and round 2 that reportedly added `.github/workflows/test.yml` to the enforcement-chain protected-gate suffix set the R-10 guard enforces -- a fact outside my read-only manifest that I did not and could not independently verify (verifying it would require reading a file outside the 6-file allowlist), but which I treat as directing context per this round's task framing. If accurate, the spec's unconditional 'none of which are protected-gate files' claim and the direct-write plan built on it (T-001/T-005, AC-027) now conflict with the file's actual guard status: an agent attempting the AC-027 direct write would be blocked by the same R-10 mechanism REQ-003 explicitly routes the four protected reviewer files around, yet the spec defines no equivalent scratchpad/human-copy procedure for `.github/workflows/test.yml`. This is a workflow-boundary contradiction (a claimed-unprotected file vs. its actual protected status) with no spec-provided fallback, which the task instructions classify as Critical. This belongs to spec review because it is a Requirements-level factual premise (not an implementation detail) that six of seven tasks' registration steps depend on.

Security Boundaries B1 in requirements.md claims 'v2 parsing rejects malformed supported_efforts/effort_control/risk_effort_matrix the same way v1 rejects malformed efforts' -- an explicit negative-path/rejection claim. Scanning acceptance-tests.md's full AC-001..AC-052/TEST-001..TEST-052 table, the only rejection-adjacent case is AC-013/TEST-013, which covers a v1 `--candidates-file` still requiring the `effort` field present (a required-ness check on ONE existing v1 field), not malformed shapes of the three NEW v2-only fields B1 names (empty/non-array `supported_efforts`, out-of-set `effort_control` values, malformed `risk_effort_matrix` structure). No AC or TEST asserts rejection of a registry carrying a malformed `supported_efforts`, `effort_control`, or `risk_effort_matrix`. Downstream failure: an implementer could build validation that silently accepts or defaults a malformed v2 field instead of rejecting it, and no acceptance test would catch the regression; a task/quality-gate reviewer has no criterion to test this claimed boundary against. This is the same category of gap round 1 already required fixing for Security Boundary B3 (remedied via AC-052/TEST-052) -- the same standard was not applied to B1. Major per calibration's 'high-risk claim with no planned validation surface,' not Critical since it is a data-validation robustness gap rather than an active safety/workflow violation.

requirements.md's Assumptions section's fourth bullet -- '`tests/run-all.sh`, `tests/run-all.ps1`, `.github/workflows/test.yml`, `tests/validate-repository.ps1`, and `tests/workflow-documentation.tests.sh` remain outside the protected-gate table for the duration of this feature' -- is stated as settled fact with no verification mechanism and no 'decision needed' marker, unlike the immediately adjacent A3 assumption in the same section, which models the correct pattern by explicitly stating a re-verification step at T-007 implementation time to guard against exactly this kind of drift ('in case an intervening history rewrite or revert on main were to remove it'). investigation.md cannot have resolved this assumption because it predates the round-1-to-round-2 window in which (per this round's supplied context, which I did not independently verify against files outside my allowlist) a concurrent merge reportedly changed this file's protected-gate status. This is a load-bearing assumption -- six of seven tasks' registration edits to `.github/workflows/test.yml` and `tests/run-all.sh` depend on it -- yet it carries none of the re-verification discipline the spec itself demonstrates elsewhere (A3) is available and cheap. Major: it does not by itself make the spec unreviewable, but it is unresolved and undermines multiple downstream ACs (AC-027, Main Workflows T-001/T-005) if false.

The two governance/approval boundaries the spec explicitly claims are concretely testable as written: (1) the four R-10 protected reviewer .md files' human-copy procedure -- AC-019 asserts the render script never opens these files for write and stages corrected content under specs/epic-159-pillar-c/human-copy/ with a SHA-256 manifest entry, and AC-020 asserts --check may read-compare them unattended in CI without tripping the guard, giving both the write-prohibition and the read-permission halves of this boundary independently falsifiable tests; (2) T-007/#155's release-ordering prerequisite gate -- AC-045 requires the implementation report to record a `git merge-base --is-ancestor` check against A3 and T-001..T-006 merge status, and AC-046 requires T-007 to land as a separate PR/release, both objectively verifiable. I am not extending this check to cover the `.github/workflows/test.yml` protected-gate-scope concern raised for this round, since that concern is a distinct claim (whether a boundary needs to exist at all for a file not currently named in REQ-003's protected-file list) rather than a testability gap in a boundary the spec does define; it is addressed under CONTRADICTION and ASSUMPTIONS-RESOLVABLE instead to avoid diluting this check with duplicate evidence.

requirements.md's Roles and Permissions and Main Workflows sections hand the implementation-policy/design reviewer a direct-write plan for `.github/workflows/test.yml` (AC-027, T-001, T-005) premised on that file being unprotected. Per this round's supplied context (unverified by me directly, as verification would require a read outside my 6-file allowlist), that premise may already be stale following a concurrent merge. If so, a downstream design/task reviewer inheriting this spec would have to independently discover the guard-status conflict and invent a REQ-003-equivalent workaround (scratchpad staging path, manifest format, human-copy target) for `.github/workflows/test.yml` that the spec does not specify anywhere -- exactly the 'invent missing product behavior' failure mode this check exists to catch, since the spec provides a template for this exact problem (the four reviewer files) but does not apply it to this file. The Risks section's mitigation for the adjacent 'shared registration surfaces' Medium risk (serialized per-task commits) also does not address a guard-rejection scenario, so no existing risk entry would prompt a downstream reader to notice the gap. Major: this blocks a clean design handoff on a load-bearing workflow step (used by 6 of 7 tasks) without rising to an unreviewable-spec-level Critical on this check specifically (the underlying workflow-boundary severity is already captured as Critical under CONTRADICTION).
